coredata.dc:title: In vitro photoinactivation of S. aureus and photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline by novel phthalocyanine-graphene quantum dots nano-assemblies
coredata.dc:creator: Openda, Yolande Ikala
Mgidlana, Sithi
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: A novel asymmetrical zinc (II) phthalocyanine (Pc) 4 bearing three dimethoxy groups and one carboxyl group was linked to glutathione capped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by the reaction of carboxylic acid substituent on Pc 4 with the amino group on the GQDs. On the other side, the symmetrical Pc analog 3 was linked to the same nanoparticles through π-π interactions. The as-formed nano-photosensitizers were fully characterized by spectroscopic methods and their photophysicochemical properties were investigated as well. Photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy was performed on the planktonic cells of S. aureus strain. And the results show that these nano assemblies were able to completely inhibit the metabolic activity of the resistant bacteria strain S. aureus with a 10.26 log reduction in the viable count. Again, asymmetrical Pc showed higher photocatalytic activity compared to the symmetrical complex with higher kobs and fast initial rates for the former. The photocatalysis obeyed the Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model. The target conjugates showed all the advantages of two different groups existing on a single entity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc phthalocyanines
Singlet oxygen
Graphene quantum dots
Tetracycline photodynamic antimicrobial therapy
Photocatalytic degradation

head.ce:title: In vitro photoinactivation of S. aureus and photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline by novel phthalocyanine-graphene quantum dots nano-assemblies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Makhanda, 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Makhanda, 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel asymmetrical zinc (II) phthalocyanine (Pc) 4 bearing three dimethoxy groups and one carboxyl group was linked to glutathione capped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by the reaction of carboxylic acid substituent on Pc 4 with the amino group on the GQDs. On the other side, the symmetrical Pc analog 3 was linked to the same nanoparticles through π-π interactions. The as-formed nano-photosensitizers were fully characterized by spectroscopic methods and their photophysicochemical properties were investigated as well. Photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy was performed on the planktonic cells of S. aureus strain. And the results show that these nano assemblies were able to completely inhibit the metabolic activity of the resistant bacteria strain S. aureus with a 10.26 log reduction in the viable count. Again, asymmetrical Pc showed higher photocatalytic activity compared to the symmetrical complex with higher kobs and fast initial rates for the former. The photocatalysis obeyed the Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model. The target conjugates showed all the advantages of two different groups existing on a single entity.

ce:section.ce:para: The extensive use of antibiotics in humans and animals has led to important environmental pollution. Antibiotics have been detected in soil, waste, and even drinking water [1,2]. Tetracycline (TC), a subject of this work, is an example of an antibiotic that is a water pollutant. Bacteria, commonly the Gram (+) strain S. aureus, constitute on their own another form of biological water contaminant. Generally, the greatest microbial risks come from the ingestion of water that is contaminated with microorganisms [3].

Recently, various methods such as membrane separation, physical absorption, chemical oxidation, biodegradation have been developed to eliminate pollution by TC in water [2,4,5]. Among the above methods, photocatalytic degradation shows the most potential because of its low mammalian toxicity and high efficiency [2,6–8]. Photocatalysis is an example of an advanced oxidation processes (AOP) [9], which has been presented as an alternative method for ensuring degradation and mineralization of TC. Whereas photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy (PACT) has been reported as the efficient alternative to eradicate the bacteria via the photosensitization process [10,11]. Hence photocatalysis and PACT are used in the present work for water purification purposes.

In photocatalysis and PACT, a non-toxic photocatalyst or photosensitizer (PS), gets excited with light of an appropriate wavelength and populates the triplet state where it interacts with molecular oxygen [12] to produce highly cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS). Among these ROS, singlet oxygen (1O2) is known to damage bacteria cells. Generally, the 1O2 is generated by energy transfer in the triplet state between the photocatalyst or PS and the molecular oxygen [13].

Metallophthalocyanines (MPcs) are effective photosensitizers and photocatalysts [14,15]. MPcs have unique properties thanks to their intense optical and electronic properties, high thermal and chemical stability, catalytic, photophysical, and photochemical characteristics [14,16]. The MPcs employed in this work contain Zn as heavy central metal. The transition of a photosensitizer from the singlet excited state to the triplet excited state happens with a spin-orbit coupling which usually occurs due heavy atom effect caused by the presence of various metal ions, halogen, and even oxygen atoms (the latter are present in the molecules employed in this work) in the macrocycle structure. This results in improved triplet quantum yields, and consequently, high singlet oxygen [17].

Recently, the trend in research is to elaborate conjugated systems made up of different components to provide a variety of advantages like electron transfer mechanisms and dual activity. For this reason, MPcs have been either interlinked or linked to numerous classes of biomolecules or even linked to different types of nanomaterials. Hence in the present study, we made use of glutathione (GSH) capped graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The use of GSH was to covalently link the carboxylic group (COOH) of the asymmetrical Pc to the amino group (NH2) of the GSH capped GQDs.

GQDs are a new series of nanoparticles with low toxicity, high photochemical stability, high surface reactivity, and easy functionalization [18,19] thus making them promising platforms. Recently, we reported on π-π stacked GQDs-phthalocyanines as photodynamic agents against S. aureus planktonic cells [20,21]. GQDs-MPc π-π stacked conjugates have also been employed for the degradation of 4-chlorophenol [22]. GQDs improved the activity of the MPcs. There are no reports on photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy analysis of covalently linked Pc-GQDs nanoconjugates so far. Covalent linking ensures that the components do not separate during application. To the best of our knowledge, these types of nanoplatforms have not yet been used as photocatalysts for the photodegradation of tetracycline in water. In terms of the degradation of TC, a FePc was used for its degradation in the presence of peroxymonosulfate [23]. A CuPc was also employed for photodegradation of TC when immobilized on CeO2/Bi2MoO6 [24]. In both cases the central metals were paramagnetic. MPcs with paramagnetic central atoms are considered to have short-lived triplet states [25], hence their ability to efficiently generate singlet oxygen which is required for photocatalysis is reduced. The Pc employed were also symmetrically substituted. In this work, we employ asymmetrically substituted MPc. MPcs with reduced symmetry are reported to demonstrate enhanced triplet state properties [26], which will result in improved singlet oxygen quantum yields.

Herein this research project, to fill this knowledge gap, we first covalently linked a novel asymmetrical Zn (II) phthalocyanine (4) to glutathione capped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by the means of an amide bond resulting in the formation of 4-GQDs. Then, its symmetrical derivative (3) was π-π stacked to GQDs as well to form 3@GQDs. The newly formed nanoconjugates showed energy transfer from the excited GQDs unit to the phthalocyanine core and they were also able to produce high amounts of singlet oxygen which is essential for the killing of bacteria in PACT and phototransformation of pollutants. It has been reported that cationic phthalocyanines are more effective than neutral and anionic Pcs against the Gram (−) bacteria such as E. coli [27], but neutral phthalocyanines (as employed in this work) are expected to be effective against the Gram (+) S. aureus.
All reactions were carried out under an inert atmosphere of argon using dried solvents.

Anhydrous zinc (II) acetate (Zn(OAc)2, 1,8-diazabicyclo [5.4.0]undec-7-ene (DBU), diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), anthracene-9,10-bis-methylmalonate (ADMA), N, N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), tetracycline (TC), and deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO‑d 6) were used as purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Other reagents and solvents such as dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), tetrahydrofuran (THF), 1-hexanol, and methanol were obtained from commercial suppliers and were of analytical grade and used without any further purification. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution pH 7.4 was prepared using appropriate amounts of Na2HPO4 and KH2PO4 in ultra-pure water from ELGA, Veolia water PURELAB, flex system (Marlow, UK). 4-(3,5-Dimethoxyphenoxy)phthalonitrile (1) [28], 4-carboxyphenoxyphthalonitrile (2) [29] and glutathione capped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [30] were respectively synthesized following previously reported procedures. AlPcSmix containing a mixture of differently sulfonated phthalocyanines was synthesized as reported in the literature [31] and employed as a singlet oxygen quantum yield standard in aqueous media.
Nutrient agar and agar broth were purchased from Merck and prepared as specified by the suppliers. Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 25923) was obtained from Davies Diagnostics.

FT-IR spectra were acquired on a Bruker® ALPHA FT-IR spectrometer with a universal attenuated total reflectance (ATR) sampling accessory. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. The triplet state quantum yields and lifetimes of the samples were obtained by laser flash photolysis system consisting of an LP980 spectrometer with a PMT-LP detector and an ICCD camera (Andor DH320T-25F03). The signal from the PMT detector was recorded on a Tektronix TDS3012C digital storage oscilloscope. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a time-correlated single-photon counting setup (TCSPC) (FluoTime 300, Picoquant GmbH) with a diode laser (LDH-P-670, Picoquant® GmbH, 20 MHz repetition rate, 44 ps pulse width). Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were obtained on a Varian Eclipse® spectrofluorometer using a 360–1100 nm filter, absorbance at the excitation wavelength was adjusted to ∼0.05 to reduce inner filter effects. Singlet oxygen quantum yields were done using a tunable laser system consisting of an Nd: YAG laser (355 nm, 135 mJ/4–6 ns) pumping an optical parametric oscillator (OPO, 30 mJ/3–5 ns) with a wavelength range of 420–2300 nm (NT-342B, Ekspla).

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) results were known by using a Malvern Zetasizer nano series, Nano-ZS90. Differential thermal analysis (DSC) analysis was done using a DSC 2500 TA instrument and Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out using a Perkin Elmer TGA 800 instrument.

Nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker AMX Advance 600 MHz NMR spectrometer using tetramethylsilane (TMS) as an internal reference. Mass spectra data were acquired on a Bruker Auto FLEX III Smart beam TOF/TOF mass spectrometer operated in positive ion mode using α-cyano-4-hydrocinnamic acid as a matrix.

A Metrohm Swiss 827 pH meter was used for pH measurements. HERMLE Z233M − 2 centrifuge was used for the harvesting of the bacteria cells. PRO VSM-3 Labplus Vortex mixer was used for the homogenization of the bacteria suspension. A thermostatic oven was used for incubation processes. The optical density of the bacteria was determined using the LEDETECT 96. Scan® 500 automatic color colony counter was used to evaluate the colony forming units CFU/mL of the bacteria. Irradiation for PACT studies (and phototransformation of TC) was conducted using a Modulight ML7710-680-RHO laser system with emissions at 670 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the zinc (II) phthalocyanines (3, 4), Scheme 1
ce:section.ce:para: Phthalonitrile 1 (0.796, 2.84 mmol) and phthalonitrile 2 (0.250 g, 0.95 mmol), an excess of Zn(OAc)2 (1.373 g, 7.48 mmol) and DBU (0.5 mL) were mixed in 1-hexanol (5 mL). The mixture was refluxed and stirred at 160 °C for 18 h under argon, Scheme 1 . After this time, the crude product was cooled to room temperature and hot methanol was added to precipitate out the blue solid. The crude product was purified by column chromatography packed with silica gel. The first fraction which is the tetrakis-(3, 5-dimethoxyphenoxy) phthalocyanato zinc (II), compound 3 (the A4 Pc type) was collected using THF as eluent. Then, upon increasing the polarity of the eluent system to (THF: methanol 5:1 v/v), an A3B target product which is the mono-(4-carboxylic acid phenoxy)-tris-(3, 5-dimethoxyphenoxy) phthalocyanato zinc (II), labeled as 4 was obtained. These products were afterward dried under a vacuum.

Symmetrical Pc (3) Yield (56%): IR (UATR-TWO™) ν max/cm−1: 3070 (Ar C–H), 2914-2844 (Aliph. C–H), 1591-1470 (CC, CN), 1486-1360 (C–C), 1320 (-O-CH3), 832. 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO‑d 6): δ (ppm): 7.76 (d, J = 8.3 Hz, 2H, Pc ring); 7.32 (dd, J = 7.8, 1.8 Hz, 2H, Pc ring); 7.16 (d, J = 2.2 Hz, 2H, Pc ring); 6.72 (d, J = 7.8 Hz, 1H, Pc ring); 6.69 (d, J = 8.2 Hz, 1H, Pc ring); 6.58 (d, J = 1.7 Hz, 2H, Pc ring); 6.48 (dd, J = 9.9, 1.8 Hz, 2H, Pc ring); 6.37 (dd, J = 2.5, 3.0, 8H, methoxy ring); 6.30 (dd, J = 2.2, 2.5, 4H, methoxy ring) and 3.68 (s, 24H, O–CH3). Elemental analysis: expected: C = 64.79, H = 4.08, N = 9.44, found: C = 64.31, H = 4.02, N = 9.42. MALDI TOF MS simulated for C64H48N8O12Zn: m/z: 1186.27, found m/z: 1187.37 for [M+H]+.

Asymmetrical Pc (4) Yield (33%): IR (UATR-TWO™) ν max/cm−1: 3393 (-OH), 3065 (Ar C–H), 2927-2839 (Aliph. C–H), 1712 (CO), 1591-1460 (CC, CN), 1480-1360 (C–C), 1323 (-O-CH3), 837. 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO‑d 6): δ (ppm): 11.30 (s, 1H, OH); 8,30 (d, J = 10.0 Hz, 2H, carboxyl ring); 7.80 (d, J = 8.2 Hz, 2H, Pc ring); 7.35 (d, J = 7.9 Hz, 2H, Pc ring); 7.20 (s, 4H, Pc ring); 6.74 (d, J = 8.1 Hz, 2H, Pc ring); 6.64 (d, J = 8.5 Hz, 2H, Pc ring); 6.52 (d, J = 10.2 Hz, 2H, carboxyl ring); 6.40 (s, 6H, methoxy ring); 6.30 (s, 3H, methoxy ring) and 3.72 (s, 18H, O–CH3). Elemental analysis: expected: C = 64.65, H = 3.79, N = 9.57, found: C = 64.09, H = 3.92, N = 9.66. MALDI TOF MS simulated for C63H44N8O12Zn: m/z: 1170.49, found m/z: 1171.33 for [M+H]+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the nanoconjugates 3@GQDs and 4-GQDs, Scheme 2
ce:section.ce:para: The nanoconjugate 3@GQDs was obtained using π–π stacking interactions procedure previously reported in the literature [32] as follows: 10 mg of Pc 3 and 5 mg of GQDs were dissolved in dry DMF (2 mL), then the mixture was sonicated for 4 h before leaving it to stir for 96 h at room temperature. The desired conjugate was obtained by precipitation method using ethanol and then dried under vacuum.

The covalent bond (π-π stacking interactions are also possible) formation between the asymmetric Pc 4 and the nanoparticle, Scheme 2 was achieved as follows [33]: briefly, 4 (10 mg) was dissolved in dry DMF (5 mL) and DCC (5 mg, 0.024 mmol) was added to activate COOH group on the Pc and thereafter the mixture was left stirring for 48 h at room temperature. Afterward, the GQDs nanoparticles (5 mg) were added to the mixture and the mixture was stirred for another 48 h. At the end of this period, the reaction mixture was washed three times with ethanol using centrifugation followed by drying under vacuum.
Fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF) were determined using comparative methods described in the literature [34]. Unsubstituted ZnPc in DMSO was used as a standard with ΦF = 0.20 [35].

The triplet quantum yield (ΦT) values for studied compounds were determined using comparative methods described in the literature [36]. For triplet state studies, solutions were introduced into a 1 cm path length UV–Visible spectrophotometric cell and de-aerated using argon for 30 min. Thereafter the solutions (standard or sample) were each sealed and illuminated using an appropriate excitation wavelength (the crossover wavelength of the sample and the standard, which was ∼620 nm). The absorbance of the sample and the standard were ∼1.5 at Q-band. The maximum triplet absorption wavelength was determined from the transient curve. Triplet lifetimes were determined by exponential fitting of the kinetic curves using the ORIGIN® 8 Professional software. ZnPc was used as the standard with the value: ΦT = 0.65 in DMSO [36].

Singlet oxygen quantum yield (ΦΔ) values were determined under ambient conditions using DPBF as a singlet oxygen quencher in DMSO, using equations described formerly [37,38]. ZnPc was used as a standard in DMSO with (ΦΔ (std) = 0.67) [38]. The absorbances of DPBF were spectroscopically monitored at 417 nm at 30 s intervals . AlPcSmix (ΦΔ = 0.42 [38]) was used as a standard in 1% DMSO aqueous solution and using ADMA as a singlet oxygen quencher.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocytotoxic activity of compounds against bacterial cells
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial cytotoxic activity was analyzed on a Gram-negative bacteria strain, S. aureus as follows: each bacterial strain was firstly inoculated in nutrient broth (5 mL) and left to grow overnight in an incubator at 37 °C to the optical density of 0.6–0.8 at 620 nm. After this, they were harvested through centrifugation using phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4), then the obtained pellets were further diluted to 1/1000 in PBS to make a stock with dilution 10−2. From this stock, 10−4, 10−5, 10−6, 10−7, and 10−8 dilution factors were prepared using serial dilutions and 100 μL of each were aseptically inoculated on agar plates in triplicate and left to incubate for 18 h. The colony counting showed that 10−5 bacteria dilution was ideal to work with as it had approximately 248 colonies (nearly 109 CFU/mL, where CFU is colony-forming units).

For the photodynamic bioassays, 100 μg/mL stock solution for each sample was prepared in 1% DMSO, from which different concentrations of 0.63, 1.25, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, and 40 μg/mL of the photosensitizers were made to determine the optimal concentration to be used in this study.

For irradiation, 2.5 mL containing respective amounts of both the sample and bacteria were firstly incubated in a shaking incubator for 30 min in the dark at 37 °C. Afterward, these suspensions were transferred to a 24-well plate which was irradiated for 2 h with 30 min intervals using a Modulight laser lamp with wavelength (λ exc∼670 nm, with constant irradiance of 524 mW cm−2 and dose of 943 J cm−2). 100 μL from each well were aseptically spread onto nutrient agar plates (in triplicate) and incubated at 37 °C overnight before counting CFUs. This experiment was also performed for the non-irradiated samples to determine their dark toxicity while the control assays consisted of PBS with DMSO only without the addition of the photosensitizers.

To phototransform TC, we employed the Modulight® Medical Laser system (ML7710-670), which has cylindrical output channels, a focusing beam, an integrated calibration module, a hand-tuning pedal, fiber sensors, connectors, and safety interlocks. A small magnetic stirrer and a sample holder make up the apparatus. The sample was exposed to a light source with a 670 nm excitation wavelength and an irradiance power of 80 mW/cm for 150 min, with spectral readings taken every 5 min.

Three independent (n = 3) experiments were conducted in triplicates and the obtained data were compared using a 3-way factorial ANOVA. The data are presented as means ± standard deviation (SD). A p-value of 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

The symmetrical and asymmetrical ZnPcs 3 and 4 were synthesized by mixed cyclotetramerization of substituted phthalonitriles 1 and 2, respectively, where we employed a ratio 3:1 of 1 and 2, respectively, in the presence of zinc acetate and DBU as an organic base in boiling 1-hexanol under an argon atmosphere (Scheme 1). The obtained mixtures of Pcs were separated by column chromatography on silica gel using gradient elution with THF (for the symmetrical 3) and 5:1 v/v THF: methanol (for the asymmetrical Pc 4).

All the synthesized compounds were characterized by the combination of elemental analysis data and spectroscopic techniques such as 1H NMR, FT-IR, UV–Vis, and MS, see Figs. S1–S4 (Supporting Information) for NMR and mass spectra.

The 1H NMR spectra of Pcs 3 and 4 (Figs. S3 and S4 in the supporting information, SI) presented similar patterns within close chemical shifts whereas the aromatic protons slightly shifted downfield in the range of 8.30-6.30 ppm and the methoxy protons at 3.68 and 3.72 ppm for 3 and 4 respectively. In addition, a broad singlet peak is observed at 11.30 ppm (Fig. S4) in the spectrum of 4. This can be assigned to the proton of the carboxylic acid group (-COOH) as it presents H-bonding characteristics. All the NMR data agreed with the proposed structures as all the substituents and ring protons appeared in their respective regions.

The mass spectra of novel complexes were obtained and their molecular ion peaks were found to be m/z 1187.37 as [M+H]+ for 3 (Fig. S1) and m/z 1171.33 as [M+H]+ for 4 (Fig. S2), which correspond to the simulated masses of both complexes respectively, generated using isotopic distribution model. The elemental analysis data also the expected percentage values for C, H, and N elements in the synthesized compounds.

It is necessary to point out that the functionalization of GQDs with GSH was done to provide NH2 groups for covalent linking with the Pc (4). Hence, the carboxylic group of Pc 4 was activated for linking with the NH2 from GQDs via an amide bond to form the 4-GQDs conjugate (Scheme 2). π-π stacking interactions are also possible for 4-GQDs. In the meantime, 3@GQDs was obtained by taking advantage of the presence of sp2 carbon on the GQDs surface which enables for physical interactions such as π-π interactions with highly aromatic macrocycles such as Pcs.

In the UV–vis spectra, phthalocyanines absorb light in the UV–visible region with absorption bands observed around 350 nm and 700 nm, known as the Soret and Q bands, respectively [39]. The maximum absorption bands were observed at 354 and 679 nm for 3 and 361 and 682 nm for 4 in DMSO, Table 1 . As shown in Fig. 1 A and Table 1, the absorption maximum of 4 is red shifted to 682 nm compared to 679 nm of 3, which is consistent with literature that states that the asymmetry in the chemical structure of asymmetrical Pcs leads to unique properties differing them from the symmetrical ones. The electron donating ability of the carboxyl group is better than that of a methoxyl group thus leading to absorption at a longer wavelength for complex 4 [40]. The UV–Vis spectrum of the GQDs shows absorption peaks at 354 m (Fig. 1A) which can be attributed to n→π* in CO [41].

Absorption spectra of the π-π stacked and amide-bonded nanohybrids are shown in Fig. 1A. It is noteworthy that there were no significant shifts in the Q bands of 3 and 4 following conjugation, but there was an increase in absorption in the region around where the GQDs absorb confirming the presence of both ZnPcs and GQDs.

Fig. 1B used as an example illustrates the spectra of the 4-GQDs conjugate in 1% DMSO. The Q-band is split due to aggregation with the low energy band is due to the monomer and the higher energy band near 650 nm (Table 1) being due to aggregation [39].

The Pcs loading onto the conjugates was investigated following a literature report [42] using absorption instead of fluorescence. This involves comparing the Q band absorbance intensity of the Pc in the conjugate with that of the free Pc. The Pc loading was ∼186 of 3/mg of 3@GQDs and ∼198 of 4/mg of 4-GQDs. GQDS as most carbon-based nanoparticles contain sp2 hybridization that gives them great ability to form π-π interactions with other π electron-rich molecules such as Pcs [43,44]. Thus, the higher loading obtained for 4-GQDs can be supported by the fact that in this conjugate, not only the covalent bonds (amide bond) are formed but there is also a possibility of Pc 4 to π-π stack on the surface of the GQDs.

The FT-IR spectra are illustrated in Fig. 2 . These spectra showed the functional groups on ZnPcs, nanoparticles, and the respective conjugates. Following the cyclotetramerization reaction of compounds 1 and 2, the –CN characteristic peak ∼2231 cm−1 was absent in the spectra of Pcs 3 and 4, suggesting the successful synthesis of the Pcs. The vibrational stretches for carboxylic OH were observed around 3393 cm−1, aromatic-CH at 3065 cm−1, aliphatic –CH at 2927-2839 cm−1, –CO at 1712 cm−1, –CC and –CN groups between 1591 and 1460 cm−1 and the -O-CH3 group at 1323 cm−1 in the case of Pc 4. In the spectrum for 3, the vibrational peaks for aromatic-CH were obtained at 3070 cm−1 and for aliphatic –CH groups were between 2914 and 2844 cm−1, –CC and –CN groups between 1591-1467 and the -O-CH3 group at 1320 cm−1. The peaks appearing in the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs at 3339, 3061-2935, 2881, 2610, and 1702 cm−1 can be attributed to –OH and –NH2; aromatic and aliphatic –CH, –SH and –CO groups, respectively.

In the spectrum of 4-GQDs , the carbonyl vibrational stretch appeared at a lower frequency of 1708 cm−1, indicating the successful formation of the amide bond. In addition, broader new shoulder stretches at 3323 cm−1 show up with increased intensity.

The DLS plots are shown in Fig. 3 where an average size distribution in solution for GQDs, 3@GQDs, and 4-GQDs were 8.7, 30.6, and 38.1 nm, respectively (Table 1). It is noteworthy that there are possibilities of 4 to also π-π stack on the GQDs surface in addition to the covalent bonds, thus giving a larger size of 4-GQDs. Size increase of nanoconjugates has been related to aggregations [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential thermal analysis (DSC)
ce:section.ce:para: TGA and DSC techniques were useful in the investigation of the thermal stability, physical and chemical phase transitions in the decomposition of the as-formed nanoconjugates.

From the TGA curves depicted in Fig. 4 A, the nanoconjugates presented the highest stability with increasing temperature; 41% and 5% weight loss for 3@GQDs and 4-GQDs, respectively at 600 °C where 80% of the graphene structure of GQDs decomposes. The higher stability of conjugate 4-GQDs might result from the high Pc loading as Pcs are reported to stabilize the thermal properties of compounds [45]. Generally, the decrease in mass observed in the region between 50 and 100 °C is due to the loss in moisture. The decrease observed around 297 °C is associated with the degradation of glutathione that was used to cap the GQDs.

Similar trends were also observed in the DSC plots (Fig. 4B). The thermograms showed exotherms centered around 50–150 °C which are attributed to the degradation of unstable organic materials, solvents, and the evaporation of moisture present on the graphene surface. GQDs exhibit a significant endothermic peak at 200 °C due to the presence of glutathione, which has been reported in the literature [46]. A similar peak is observed in 3-GQDs this may be attributed to free GSH present around the GQDs [47]. However, the peak is not present in 4-GQDs, this implies that all the available binding site carboxylic moiety have been consumed and the chemical bond (amide) is formed. In addition, the endothermic peak observed above 350 °C in the thermogram of 4-GQDs can be related to the initiation of the decomposition of chemical groups covalently linked to the edges of the carbon backbone of GQDs [48,49].

To further characterize the optical properties, the fluorescence behaviors of the symmetric and asymmetric Pcs as well as their prepared GQDs-conjugate analogs were studied in DMSO at room temperature. Firstly, the fluorescence spectra were recorded by exciting at a wavelength around where the Pcs absorb (λ exc 610 nm). Figs. 5 A and B and S5 (in supporting Information) show typical absorbance, excitation, and emission bands of phthalocyanines and their conjugates. The Stokes shifts were small, Table 1, typical of phthalocyanines [50]. The characteristic emission peaks of respective conjugates appeared as a single-broad band at 690 nm for 3@GQDs and 691 nm for 4-GQDs, Table 1. GQDs alone did not exhibit any emission band at this excitation wavelength (610 nm).

Generally, the calculated fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF) were low thanks to the presence of zinc metaionson in the core of the Pcs which supports intersystem crossing to the triplet excited state rather than fluorescence [17]. ΦF value for 4 is reduced compared to the symmetrical analog 3 (at ΦF = 0.25 and 0.22 for 3 and 4, respectively). As reported before, this could be due to self-quenching or intersystem crossing [51].

The ΦF values decreased to 0.18 and 0.14 for 3@GQDs and 4-GQDs, respectively, compared to 3 and 4 alone. Electron donating groups are known to increase intersystem crossing in porphyrins-like complexes [52]. The lower value obtained in the case of 4-GQDs could results from the higher Pcs loading as compared to 3@GQDs. The high Pc loading could lead to aggregation, resulting in quenching of fluorescence.

Using the TCSPC technique (Fig. 6 ) the fluorescence lifetimes were determined, and the values were in agreement with the reported values for most monomeric phthalocyanines [53] as all complexes and conjugates had fluorescence lifetimes (τF) between 2.15 and 3.10 ns. The fluorescence lifetimes decreased with the decrease in ΦF since the two are directly associated.

The emission spectra of the GQDs alone and studied conjugates were also recorded in DMSO at room temperature at λ exc 340 nm (where GQDs absorb). A characteristic emission band is observed at 453 nm for GQDs itself (Fig. 5C). In this case, the ΦF = 0.27 [32] for the GQDs alone yet this value considerably decreased in the conjugates as they were found to be up to 3 times lower than the GQDs itself (ΦF = 0.093 and 0.087 for 3@GQDs and 4-GQDs, respectively). This decrease suggests that there could be Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) causing a fluorescence quenching of the GQDs (donor) and by the Pc complexes (acceptor) and other energy transfer processes which have been reported to deactivate the emission of the GQDs. Amongst them, there is photoinduced energy transfer (PET) which occurs when only one of the components changes its spectral characteristics, the GQDs in this case [54,55].
We herein made use of the luminescence properties of GQDs to study their energy transfer process in the ground and/or excited state in the presence of phthalocyanines.

The FRET efficiency (Eff) was determined experimentally [55] by applying equation (1) using the ΦF of GQDs in the presence and absence of Pcs:

The efficiencies of energy transfer (Eff) reported in Table 1 indicate that there is a similar spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of GQDs and absorption spectra of the Pcs as observed in Fig. 5C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triplet quantum yields (ɸT) and triplet lifetimes (τT)
ce:section.ce:para: Triplet quantum yields indicate the number of molecules that undergo intersystem crossing (ISC) to populate the triplet state. An example of triplet absorption decay curve is given in Fig. 7 , the triplet state quantum yields, and lifetimes are shown in Table 1. Of the Pcs alone, a higher ɸT value belonged to the asymmetric Pc derivatives (4) as asymmetry is known to introduce distortions [56], resulting in faster intersystem crossing to the triplet state, consequently increasing the triplet state population.

The trend observed for MPc containing heavy central metals is that they have high triplet quantum yields resulting in low triplet lifetimes. However, long triplet lifetimes are important to ensure that the energy transfer from the triplet excited state to ground state molecular oxygen is efficient. Triplet lifetimes decreased with an increase in triplet quantum yields upon conjugation as expected [25].

Singlet oxygen (1O2) is one of the reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated when energy transfer occurs between the triplet state of the photosensitizer and ground-state molecular oxygen during an irradiation process. The amount of 1O2 formed during this process is quantified as singlet oxygen quantum yield (ΦΔ).
By the means of a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, the decreases in DPBF (in DMSO) and ADMA (in 1% DMSO) absorbances at 417 and 380 nm, respectively, were monitored for all the compounds (Figs. 8 A and B).

The ΦΔ followed the same trend as with ΦT values. The obtained ΦΔ values for 3 and 4 were 0.58 and 0.65 respectively in DMSO. An overall increase in singlet oxygen quantum yield was observed for the conjugates (ΦΔ = 0.64 for 3@GQDs and ΦΔ = 0.75 for 4-GQDs). The increased ΦΔ values obtained in the presence of GQDs correspond to the increase in triplet quantum yields since ΦΔ results from the interaction of the latter with the molecular oxygen.

The increase in the ΦΔ value of 3 and 4 in the presence of GQDs, (Table 1)s could be due to the presence of electron-donating groups on GQDs. As stated above,electron-donating groups are known to increase intersystem crossing to the triplet state in porphyrin-like complexes [52]. Another reason is that the increase in π bonds in the nanoconjugate can increase ΦΔ [57,58].

In 1% DMSO, the ΦΔ values for 3, 3@GQDs and 4, 4-GQDs are reduced in water compared to DMSO, Table 1. This could be due to the reported quenching effect of water on the singlet oxygen generation of phthalocyanine [35,59].

A comparative study on the photoactivity of the prepared photosensitizers was assessed and log reduction values were calculated using the CFU values. The obtained results are presented in Figs. 9 and 10
and Table 2 .

Generally, the nanoconjugates exhibited significantly increased in vitro antibacterial photodynamic action toward drug-resistant Gram-positive S. aureus strain, superior to that of the free Pcs under the same working conditions, Fig. 10.

4-GQDs conjugate displays significant activity with 10.26 log unit reduction (0.49% of surviving bacteria) at 10 μg/mL under 90 min light irradiation. 3@GQDs on the other side caused a reduction of 10.26 log10 CFU in the viable count at an extended irradiation time of 120 min, Table 2. The higher activity of 4-0GQDs (same log reduction as 3@GQDs, but 4-GQDs at a shorter time) is due to the higher singlet oxygen quantum yield in 4-GQDs. The Pcs alone (3 and 4) show log10 reduction of 1.57 and 2.15, respectively (2.65 and 1.20% survival respectively) even with increased irradiation time Figs. 10A and B, Table 2. The huge increase in the activity of the nanoconjugate presents new avenues towards the use of GQDs-based drugs in PACT. In addition, the dark studies of all the compounds showed no activity as they were comparable to the control groups (see Fig. 9). .

To test the photocatalytic degradation performance of GQDs, Pcs, and their nanocomposites, TC (structure shown as an insert in Fig. 11 ) was employed as a model contaminant in water. The goal of this study was to develop heterogeneous photocatalysts. Potocatalytic reactions were carried out in a specially built photoreactor using light from Modulight (λ = 670 nm), at room temperature. To establish equilibrium, five different concentrations of TC were prepared and equilibrated overnight with Pc or nanocomposite or GQDs (6.0 mg). Thereafter, photocatalytic tests were carried out at 5 min intervals under visible light irradiation.

When TC is irradiated in the absence of Pc complexes and nanoconjugates, there were no changes in its UV–visible spectra. In addition, there was no degradation in the dark. Fig. 11 depicts the spectral changes that occur during the photodegradation of TC when exposed to visible light, confirming that the employed catalysts were photoactive. The spectra showed distinctive bands attributed to functional groups of TC in aqueous solution as reported before [60,61]. Carbonyls and enolic groups of TC were assigned to the two absorption bands of TC in water at 275 and 357 nm, respectively. Degradation of TC at both 275 and 357 nm was observed as irradiation time increases. A decrease in intensity is due to effectively reduced enolic groups and the formation of intermediates [62,63].

The photocatalysts were used to degrade five different concentrations of TC, 2.90

M. The plots for ln (Co/C) vs irradiation time were linear, and thus followed pseudo-first-order model, Fig. 12 . It has been found that photodegradation of dyes by Pcs alone and when paired with nanomaterials follows a pseudo-first-order model [64,65].

Initial rates and rate constants (kobs) of photodegradation decreased with increasing TC concentrations, while the half-life rose, Table 3 . This means that the photocatalytic activity of the employed photocatalysts decreases as the amount of antibiotics in water increases. The conjugates showed fast photodegradation rates and higher rate constants (kobs) while the GQDs showed the least. The asymmetrical complex 4 has a higher photocatalytic activity than complex 3. Since complex 4 and conjugates generate high singlet oxygen quantum yield, the observed results confirm that photocatalytic activity is directly related to singlet oxygen.

Langmuir-Hinshel-wood model was employed heterogeneous photocatalytic degradation. It is given by equation given by equation (2) [66]:

where ro represents the initial photocatalytic degradation rate (mol. L−1 min−1), Co represents the initial TC concentration, k represents the apparent reaction rate constant (mol. L−1 min−1), and KA represents the adsorption coefficient. k and KA were respectively obtained from the intercept and slope of the plot 1/ro vs 1/Co, Fig. 13 .

Again, following Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetics model studies, it was observed that complex 4 and its conjugate with GQDs exhibit higher KA values and k values than 3 and its conjugate, Table 4 . This indicates that when the photocatalytic activity improves, so does the adsorption. Due to the increased photocatalytic activity of conjugates, more TC adsorbed on the conjugate can be phototransformed, effectively under visible light irradiation.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized and characterized novel Pcs and nano-sized assemblies based on the GQDs capped with glutathione. The studied nanoconjugates effectively treated antibiotic-resistant bacteria: S. aureus. We also proved the utility of PACT with the aid of well-designed photosensitizers for combating bacteria without the concern of resistance development that occurs in antibiotics. Therefore, currently studied nanoconjugates may hold great potential as bactericidal agents for other photodynamic applications. The quenching process of GQDs emission was elucidated to be based on the Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism between the GQDs and zinc Pcs in a donor-acceptor relationship. In photocatalysis, Pc complexes conjugates, and nanoparticles showed that they are potential candidates for photodegradation of tetracycline in water. The improved photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposites is due to their capacity to generate more singlet oxygen.
